It reminded her of the woods and of the cabin where her father had taught her to read by candlelight. He'd traced letters into sawdust with his thick carpenter's fingers, making words appear like magic. She was lucky to know how to read. There wasn't much else to do while on duty. She hoped to receive some more books in the next shipment. Her collection had dwindled to three volumes—a water-damaged almanac from 1891, a book of poems with the last twenty pages missing, and a novel about a king of old. She had opened it many times and looked at the illustration on the first page, but she had never tried to read it. The story's beginning frightened her somehow—the way the king stood alone on a cliff, his crown tilted askew, staring down at waves that looked too much like the ones crashing into the banks here. The longer she stared at the drawing, the more it drew her in. She had once looked at it for so long, she had forgotten to wind the lighthouse mechanism, and the beam had gone dark for nearly an hour. When she'd finally torn her eyes away, her vision swam with afterimages that took days to dissipate. Ever since then, just thinking about the book made her lose track of time, then she had to check the logbook to remind. herself what day it was. But the logbook had become unreliable too—whole weeks appeared twice, dates jumped backward, and her own handwriting looked unfamiliar and wrong. Thinking about it made her dizzy, so she sat down and tried to orient herself. What date was it today? She wrote "November 14, 1910" on a scrap of paper, then paused. Had she already lived this day?
